content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

August 20, 2012


Kevin B. Briggs
County Counsel
2220 Tulare Street, Suite 500
Fresno, CA 93721-2128

Re:     Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-12-103

Dear Mr. Briggs:

     This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Fresno County Supervisor Judy Case regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

     Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

     May Supervisor Case take part in discussions and decisions of the Fresno County Board of Supervisors regarding approval or denial of the Cemex appeal and certifying its E.I.R. when the result of the decisions on the Cemex project could affect her economic interests?

CONCLUSION

     Supervisor Case may not participate in decisions of the Board of Supervisors regarding approval or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. because there will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her source of income.

___________________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-12-103
Page No. 2

FACTS

This is a follow-up request to the advice letter issued to you on behalf of Supervisor Judy
Case on May 22, 2012. Supervisor Case and her husband have provided additional facts for this
request.

The subject of your previous request for advice was an imminent appeal to the Fresno
County Board of Supervisors by Cemex of a decision by the Fresno County Planning
Commission denying Cemex's application for a conditional use permit. The appeal has now
been filed with the Fresno County Board of Supervisors and is expected to be heard on
August 28, 2012.

Cemex is seeking approval to mine Jesse Morrow Mountain in Fresno County. The mine
would produce rock and gravel (aggregate) and Cemex would produce and sell the products to
private companies, individuals, cities, counties, and districts. According to the E.I.R. for the
Cemex project, which must also be certified by the Board of Supervisors, the Cemex project will
generate hundreds of truck trips per day and will produce air pollution as a result of those trips
and the mining operations.

Supervisor Case's husband, Mr. McNairy, is a Certified Public Accountant and owns his
own accounting business. Supervisor Case has a community property interest in income from
the firm. Supervisor Case has reported clients of Mr. McNairy's firm as sources of income to
her on her last Statement of Economic Interests (Form 700). Each client was a source of income
of over $10,000 or more to her in the previous year. Among the sources were (1) Roger Van
Groningen, (2) Van-G trucking, and (3) Sanger Cold Storage.

Supervisor Case's investment interest in her husband's firm is currently valued at
approximately $200,000. The income to this business was also approximately $200,000 in the
most recent fiscal year (this amount does not include rental costs because Supervisor Case and
her husband own the building in which this business operates and pay themselves rent from the
business) making Supervisor Case's one-half community property share of this income
$100,000.

Mr. Van Groningen is the President and owner of Van-G Logistics and owns Van-G
Trucking, Sanger Cold Storage and another cold storage facility in the City of Selma. Van-G
Logistics is a statewide trucking operation with five locations across California, including two
cold storage sites in Fresno County.

Mr. Van Groningen intends to expand a rail distribution center near Selma on 154 acres
located at the corner of East Conejo Avenue and South Peach Avenue. The County of Fresno,
through its Board of Supervisors on August 9, 2011, approved a general plan amendment that
would allow Mr. Van Groningen to expand the rail distribution center. Supervisor Case recused
herself from that decision without seeking advice from the FPPC.

File No. A-12-103
Page No. 3


The Van Groningen General Plan Amendment allows, by right, various uses including concrete and cement product production as well as ready-mix concrete production. The Van Groningen General Plan Amendment imposes various requirements on the inception of such uses, including a Site Plan Review, landscaping plans and provisions subject to County approval, the irrevocable dedication of a right of way for a future street, and the requirement to participate in the funding of on and off-site traffic improvements. The cost on site improvements are estimated in excess of $448,000. The costs of future off-site improvements are estimated at $105,200. Thus a lot of development costs must be incurred before Van Groningen can begin distributing products from that location.

Mr. Van Groningen has been quoted in the local press as saying that if the rail distribution center is expanded by adding one 9,000 foot track:

1. The rail distribution center will be able to load 60 cars of fresh fruit and vegetables, and unload 100 cars of aggregate per day.

2. The expansion of the rail distribution center could begin bringing in aggregate by rail in 90 days.

3. The rail distribution center has the potential to put off the mining of aggregate at the Jesse Morrow Mountain.

4. Transporting aggregate by rail from several locations within a 400 mile radius could supply all of Fresno County with all the aggregate it will ever need, providing no need to go forward with the Jesse Morrow Mountain mining because of this alternative.

5. He already has suppliers in place.

6. He does not have a stance for or against the Cemex mine.

7. Van-G Logistics could in 6-9 months schedule trains carrying 80-100 cars of aggregates on a continual basis and use of rail would save money, reduce carbon dioxide and reduce wear and tear on roads.

If the rail distribution center were to be expanded and additional aggregate were brought in by rail Mr. McNairy's firm's income would increase by an estimated $10,000 per fiscal year due to additional business from Mr. Van Groningen.

You ask if Supervisor Case would have a conflict of interest in voting upon the appeal to the Fresno County Board of Supervisors of a Planning Commission Decision to either grant or deny Cemex's application when Supervisor Case has an economic interest in Mr. Van Groningen and his companies. The success of the Cemex application could affect Mr. Van Groningen's rail distribution center project in that if the Cemex application is approved, the rail distribution center project would become less useful. However, you have also said that the rail distribution center

File No. A-12-103
Page No. 4


expansion could become part of the Cemex decision, as it could be considered as an alternative
to the Cemex proposal.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) As discussed in our prior letter to you dated May 22, 2012, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a
disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. As we noted then, Supervisor
Case is a public official who will be making a governmental decision regarding the Cemex
appeal. Her relevant economic interests that would be at issue are her sources of income,
business interests, and personal finances, as described in Step 3, below.

Step Three: What are the official's economic interests?

Of the economic interests recognized under the Act,² those interests implicated by your
account of the facts are the following:

Sources of Income and Business Interests – A public official has an economic interest in
any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) You have stated that
Supervisor Case has received $500 or more from her husband's business and the clients of her
husband's business, and she has listed them as sources of income on her Statement of Economic
Interests. Among those clients are Roger Van Groningen and his respective businesses, which
represent sources of income to the Supervisor.

Personal Finances – A public official will always have an economic interest in his or her
personal finances. A governmental decision will have an effect on an official's economic
interest in his or her personal finances if the decision will result in the personal expenses, income,
assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or
decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.)

Step Four: Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the
decision?

Business Interests and Sources of Income – Regulation 18704.1(a) states that a person,
including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved in a decision before the
official's agency when that source of income, either directly or by an agent:

² Our analysis is limited to the economic interests you have identified.

File No. A-12-103
Page No. 5

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;

"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or
revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the
subject person."

Such interests that are not directly involved in governmental decisions under the rules
quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704(a) and 18704.1(b).)

The decision regarding the Cemex project was not initiated by Supervisor Case's sources
of income or by her husband's business, nor are they parties in, or the subject of, the proceeding
within the meaning of Regulation 18704.1. Barring additional facts, it appears that Roger Van
Groningen, his businesses and Mr. McNairy's business are indirectly involved in the Cemex
appeal decision.

You have stated that it is possible that the expansion of the rail distribution center could
become an alternative to the mining of Jesse Morrow Mountain. Should that become the case,
Mr. Van Groningen would become the subject of the decision and he would be a directly
involved source of income in the decision and a different analysis than set forth below would
apply. However, under the facts as you have described them, that is not presently the case.

Personal Finances – An official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is
deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect
on the economic interest. (Regulation 18704.5.) If Mr. Van Groningen's project becomes part
of the decision, it does appear that the success or failure of the Cemex project would affect Roger
Van Groningen and his business. Even if Roger Van Groningen and/or his businesses do not
become part of the decision, it is likely that the success or failure of the Cemex appeal would
affect his business. Additionally, the amount of business that Mr. Van Groningen has may
increase or decrease his need for accounting services being provided by Mr. McNairy's firm.
Therefore, Supervisor Case's personal finances would be directly involved in the decision
because the income her and her husband receive from her husband's accounting firm would be
affected.

Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect
on the official's economic interests?

Materiality

Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality
standard appropriate to each economic interest.

File No. A-12-103
Page No. 6


Sources of Income - Regulation 18705.3 provides that the effect of a decision is material as to an
individual who is an indirectly involved source of income to an official if any of the following
applies:

"(A) The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other tangible or
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or

(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that is
considered material under Title 2, California Code of Regulations, sections 18705.2(b)."

If the Cemex application were approved or denied and the rail distribution center were to
be expanded as has already been approved, it would appear that Roger Van Groningen's income,
investments, or other intangible assets or liabilities would be affected by $1,000 or more because
failure of the Cemex application would likely increase business to him by at least that much.
Therefore, the effect on Mr. Van Groningen would be material.

If the Cemex decision does involve Mr. Van Groningen or his businesses directly, in that
the rail distribution center plan becomes an alternative to the Cemex application, the effect of the
decision would be material, as any effect on a source of income that is directly involved in a
governmental decision is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.3(a).)

Business Entities - The materiality standard for a business that is indirectly involved in a
governmental decision can be found in Regulation 18705.1. The net income from
Mr. McNairy's business for the previous fiscal year was $200,000. Therefore, Regulation
18705.1 provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on his business entity is
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or
more; or,

(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for
a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more."

During a telephone conversation on July 12, 2012, Mr. McNairy stated that if the
Cemex application were denied and the rail distribution expansion were completed the
increase in his firm's gross revenues due to additional accounting services for
Mr. Van Groningen would be approximately $10,000 each fiscal year. The decision
would not result in the firm incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or

File No. A-12-103
Page No. 7


eliminating existing expenses and the decision would not result in an increase or decrease
in the value of the firm's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more. Therefore, there would
not be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Mr. McNairy's firm as the
result of the decision to approve or deny the Cemex application you have described
provided the $10,000 increase in revenues is a correct estimation of the expected increase
in revenues to the firm.

Personal Finances – The materiality standard for an effect on an official's personal
finances is stated in Regulation 18705.5(a), which provides:

"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal finances
is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period. When determining
whether a governmental decision has a material financial effect on a public
official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial
effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official,
nor a financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and
liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect
investment interest shall be considered." [Emphasis added.]

Foreseeability

Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his
or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is "reasonably foreseeable"
that the standard will be met. A material financial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be met
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain
to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re
Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is
made depends on facts and circumstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra, at 198.)
Because the Commission does not act as a finder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby,
supra), the foreseeability of a particular financial effect is a determination that must be left, in
most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official.

Based upon the facts you provided, we believe it is reasonably foreseeable that the
Cemex decision will have a material financial effect upon Supervisor Case's economic interests
in her source of income. Therefore, Supervisor Case should not participate in the Cemex
decision.

File No. A-12-103
Page No. 8

Steps Seven and Eight: Does this governmental decision come within any exception
to the conflict-of-interest rules?

Public Generally & Legally Required Participation

Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally.
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).)

Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section
87101; Regulation 18708.)

However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Supervisor Case's circumstances, so
we will not address them further.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Sukhi K. Brar
Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

the result of the decisions on the Cemex project could affect her economic interests?

approval or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. because there will be a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her source of income.

sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within 
the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic interests. (Section 87103; 
Regulation 18700(a).) As discussed in our prior letter to you dated May 22, 2012, the 
Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding whether an individual has a 
disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. As we noted then, Supervisor 
Case is a public official who will be making a governmental decision regarding the Cemex 
appeal. Her relevant economic interests that would be at issue are her sources of income, 
business interests, and personal finances, as described in Step 3, below. 
Step Three: What are the official's economic interests? 
Of the economic interests recognized under the Act,2·those interests implicated by your 
account of the facts are the following: 
Sources of Income and Business Interests - A public official has an economic interest in 
any source of income, including promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within 12 
months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c); Regulation 18703.3.) You have stated that 
Supervisor Case has received $500 or more from her husband• s business and the clients of her 
husband's business, and she has listed them as sources of income on her Statement of Economic 
Interests. Among those clients are Roger Van Groningen and his respective businesses, which 
represent sources of income to the Supervisor. 
Personal Finances - A public official will always have an economic interest in his or her · 
personal finances. A governmental decision will have an effect on an official's economic 
interest in his or her personal finances if the decision will result in the personal expenses, 
income, assets or liabilities of the official or his or her immediate family increasing or 
decreasing. (Section 87103; Regulation 18703.5.) 
Step Four: Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in the 
decision? 
Business Interests and Sources of Income - Regulation 18704.1 (a) states that a person, 
including business entities and sources of income, is directly involved in a decision before the 
official's agency when that source of income, either directly or by an agent: 
2 Our analysis is limited to the economic interests you have identified.  
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an 
application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; 
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the 
decision before the official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a 
proceeding if a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or 
revocation of any lidnse, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
subject person." 
Such interests that are not directly involved in governmental decisions under the rules 
quoted above are regarded as indirectly involved. (Regulations 18704(a) and 18704.l(b).) 
The decision regarding the Cemex project was not initiated by Supervisor Case's sources 
of income or by her husband's business, nor are they parties in, or the subject of, the proceeding 
within the meaning of Regulation 18704.1. Barring additional facts, it appears that Roger Van 
Groningen, his businesses and Mr. McNairy's business are indirectly involved in the Cemex 
appeal decision. 
You have stated that it is possible that the expansion of the rail distribution center could 
become an alternative to the mining of Jesse Morrow Mountain. Should that become the case, 
Mr. Van Groningen would become the subject of the decision and he would be a directly 
involved source of income in the decision and a different analysis than set forth below would 
apply. However, under the facts as you have described them, that is not presently the case. 
Personal Finances - An official's economic interest in his or her personal finances is 
deemed to be directly involved in the governmental decision if facts suggest any financial effect 
on the economic interest. (Regulation 18704.5.) If Mr. Van Groningen's project becomes part 
of the decision, it does appear that the success or failure of the Cemex project would affect Roger 
Van Groningen and his business. Even if Roger Van Groningen and/or his businesses do not 
become part of the decision, it is likely that the success or failure of the Cemex appeal would 
affect his business. Additionally, the amount of business that Mr. Van Groningen has may 
increase or decrease his need for accounting services being provided by Mr. McNairy's firm. 
Therefore, Supervisor Case's personal finances would be directly involved in the decision 
because the income her and her husband receive from her husband's accounting firm would be 
affected. 
Steps Five and Six: Will there be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect 
on the official's economic interests? 
Materiality 
Having identified the economic interests involved, and determined whether each interest 
is directly or indirectly involved in the decision at issue, it is necessary to identify the materiality 
standard appropriate to each economic interest.  
Sources of Income - Regulation 18705.3 provides that the effect of a decision is material as to an 
individual who is an indirectly involved source of income to an official if any of the following 
applies: 
"(A)The decision will affect the individual's income, investments, or other tangible or 
intangible assets or liabilities (other than real property) by $1,000 or more; or 
(B) The decision will affect the individual's real property interest in a manner that is 
considered material under Title 2, California Code of Regulations, sections 18705 .2(b ). " 
If the Cemex application were approved or denied and the rail distribution center were to 
be expanded as has already been approved, it would appear that Roger Van Groningen's income, 
investments, or other intangible assets or liabilities would be affected by $1,000 or more because 
failure of the Cemex application would likely increase business to him by at least that much. 
Therefore, the effect on Mr. Van Groningen would be material. 
If the Cemex decision does involve Mr. Van Groningen or his businesses directly, in that 
the rail distribution center plan becomes an alternative to the Cemex application, the effect of the 
decision would be material, as any effect on a source of income that is directly involved in a 
governmental decision is deemed material. (Regulation 18705.J(a).) 
Business Entities - The materiality standard for a business that is indirectly involved in a 
governmental decision can be found in Regulation 18705.1. The net income from 
Mr. McNairy's business for the previous fiscal year was $200,000. Therefore, Regulation 
18705.1 provides that the financial effect of a governmental decision on his business entity is 
material if it is reasonably foreseeable that: 
(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the 
business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or 
more; or, 
(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or 
avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for 
a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or, 
(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value 
of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more." 
During a telephone conversation on July 12, 2012, Mr. McNairy stated that if the 
Cemex application were denied and the rail distribution expansion were completed the 
increase in his firm's gross revenues due to additional accounting services for 
Mr.Van Groningen would be approximately $10,000 each fiscal year. The decision 
would not result in the firm incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or  
eliminating existing expenses and the decision would not result in an increase or decrease 
in the value of the firm's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more. Therefore, there would 
not be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon Mr. McNairy's firm as the 
result of the decision to approve or deny the Cemex application you have described 
provided the $10,000 increase in revenues is a correct estimation of the expected increase 
in revenues to the firm. 
Personal Finances - The materiality standard for an effect on an official's personal 
finances is stated in Regulation 18705.5(a), which provides: 
"A reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a public official's personal fmances 
is material if it is at least $250 in any 12-month period. When detennining 
whether a governmental decision has a material.financial effect on a public 
official's economic interest in his or her personal finances, neither a financial 
effect on the value of real property owned directly or indirectly by the official, 
nor a.financial effect on the gross revenues, expenses, or value of assets and 
liabilities of a business entity in which the official has a direct or indirect 
investment interest shall be considered." [Emphasis added.] 
Foreseeability 
Once a public official has determined the materiality standard applicable to each of his 
or her economic interests, the next step is determining whether it is :•reasonably foreseeable" 
that the standard will be met. A material fmancial effect on an economic interest is "reasonably 
foreseeable" if it is substantially likely that one or more of the materiality standards will be· met 
as a result of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18706(a).) An effect need not be certain 
to be considered "reasonably foreseeable," but it must be more than a mere possibility. (In re 
Thomer (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) 
Ultimately, whether a material financial effect is foreseeable at the time a decision is 
made depends on facts and circwnstances peculiar to each case. (In re Thorner, supra, at 198.) 
Because the Commission does not act as a fmder of fact in providing advice (In re Oglesby, 
supra), the foreseeability of a particular fmancial effect is a determination that must be left, in 
most instances, to the informed judgment of the public official. 
Based upon the facts you provided, we believe it is reasonably foreseeable that the 
Cemex decision will have a material fmancial effect upon Supervisor Case's economic interests 
in her source of income. Therefore, Supervisor Case should not participate in the Cemex 
decision.  
Steps Seven and E.igbt: Does this governmental decision come within any exception 
to the conflict-of-interest rules? 
Public Generally & Legally Required Participation 
Even if an official has a conflict of interest, disqualification is not required if the 
governmental decision affects the public official's economic interests in a manner that is 
indistinguishable from the manner in which the decision will affect the public generally. 
(Section 87103; Regulation 18707(a).) 
Additionally, in certain rare circumstances, a public official may be called upon to take 
part in a decision despite the fact that the official may have a disqualifying conflict of interest 
under the "legally required participation" exception. This exception applies only in certain very 
specific circumstances where the government agency would be paralyzed from acting. (Section 
87101; Regulation 18708.) 
However, you have not presented any facts indicating that the "public generally" or the 
"legally required participation" exceptions are applicable to Supervisor Case's circumstances, so 
we will not address them further.

QUESTION: May Supervisor Case take part in discussions and decisions of the Fresno County Board 
of Supervisors regarding approval or denial of the Cemex appeal and certifying its E.I.R. when 
the result of the decisions on the Cemex project could affect her economic interests?

CONCLUSION: Supervisor Case may not participate in decisions of the Board of Supervisors regarding 
approval or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. because there will be a 
reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her source of income.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 August 20, 2012 Kevin B. Briggs County Counsel 2220 Tulare Street, Suite 500 Fresno, CA 93721-2128 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-12-103 Dear Mr. Briggs: This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Fresno County Supervisor Judy Case regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented; the Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Additionally, our advice is limited to obligations arising under the Act. We do not address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION May Supervisor Case take part in discussions and decisions of the Fresno County Board of Supervisors regarding approval or denial of the Cemex appeal and certifying its E.I.R. when the result of the decisions on the Cemex project could affect her economic interests? CONCLUSION Supervisor Case may not participate in decisions of the Board of Supervisors regarding approval or denial of the Cemex project and certifying its E.I.R. because there will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon her source of income. ___________________________ ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-12-103 Page No. 2 FACTS This is a follow-up request to the advice letter issued to you on behalf of Supervisor Judy Case on May 22, 2012. Supervisor Case and her husband have provided additional facts for this request. The subject of your previous request for advice was an imminent appeal to the Fresno County Board of Supervisors by Cemex of a decision by the Fresno County Planning Commission denying Cemex's application for a conditional use permit. The appeal has now been filed with the Fresno County Board of Supervisors and is expected to be heard on August 28, 2012. Cemex is seeking approval to mine Jesse Morrow Mountain in Fresno County. The mine would produce rock and gravel (aggregate) and Cemex would produce and sell the products to private companies, individuals, cities, counties, and districts. According to the E.I.R. for the Cemex project, which must also be certified by the Board of Supervisors, the Cemex project will generate hundreds of truck trips per day and will produce air pollution as a result of those trips and the mining operations. Supervisor Case's husband, Mr. McNairy, is a Certified Public Accountant and owns his own accounting business. Supervisor Case has a community
